PRA SS5/25 commenced on 3 December 2025 and replaces SS3/19 in its entirety [pra_ss5-25_climate__0008__3-implementation].
The two primary transmission channels are physical risks and transition risks [pra_ss5-25_climate__0006__2-how-climate-related-risks-affect-firms].

According to the PRA, climate-related risks are distinctive because they are not possible to rely on historic data series and experience available for other risks, and they present unique challenges that require a strategic approach [pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis].

The PRA expects insurers to include climate scenario analysis (CSA) in their Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA) and Stress and Scenario Testing (SST) components, unless the impact is immaterial [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. They should consider the latest climate science and advances in climate scenario modelling [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. Insurers are expected to build on scenarios selected for their SSTs to explore climate-related risks to their business model over relevant time horizons, using assumptions and parameters (e.g., sea surface temperatures, precipitation) that are sufficiently granular to stress the risks they face [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. They must specify management actions to take in different circumstances, including changes to underwriting (e.g., increased deductibles) or investment strategy (e.g., reduced exposure to certain sectors), and establish suitable trigger points [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. Insurers should also consider climate-related reputational risks, including potential litigation or 'greenwashing' claims, arising from their strategies and public commitments [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].

The PRA expects insurers to develop processes to consider the impact on capital levels of reasonably foreseeable adverse scenarios, including material climate-related risks, as part of their capital management plans and ORSA [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. Insurers should include climate scenario analysis (CSA) in their Stress and Scenario Testing (SST) component of their ORSAs, unless the impact is immaterial, and should consider the latest climate science and advances in climate scenario modelling [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. They are expected to build on scenarios selected for their SSTs to explore climate-related risks to their business model over relevant time horizons, making assumptions and building scenarios sufficiently granular to stress for specific risks (e.g., tropical storms, flooding, credit risk) and parameters (e.g., GDP, inflation) [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. Insurers should specify management actions they would take in different circumstances, including changes to underwriting or investment strategy, and establish suitable trigger points for these actions [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. They should also consider climate-related reputational risks arising from their investment and underwriting strategies, historical activities, and wider engagement on climate change [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].

Non-life insurers should consider the impact of climate change on their natural catastrophe risk, assessing whether this impact is factored into quantitative tools and making adjustments where material [pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. They should also consider how climate change might lead to larger claims than historical experience would suggest [pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. Furthermore, they should consider the potential for climate change to lead to an accumulation of claims under liability insurances, allowing for claims to emerge under multiple contracts and underwriting years [pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk].

According to Guideline 13, a policy on fit and proper requirements must include a description of the procedure for assessing the fitness and propriety of persons who effectively run the undertaking or have other key functions, both when being considered for the specific position and on an on-going basis [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0014__guideline-13-fit-and-proper-policies-and]. The policy must also describe the situations that give rise to a re-assessment of the fit and proper requirements [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0014__guideline-13-fit-and-proper-policies-and] and the fit and proper procedures for assessing other relevant personnel not subject to Article 42 of the Solvency II Directive [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0014__guideline-13-fit-and-proper-policies-and].

Guideline 11 requires that persons who effectively run the undertaking or have other key functions, including members of the administrative, supervisory or management body, be 'fit' and take account of the respective duties allocated to individual persons to ensure appropriate diversity of qualifications, knowledge and relevant experience [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements]. The members of the administrative, management or supervisory body collectively must possess appropriate qualification, experience and knowledge about at least: insurance and financial markets; business strategy and business model; system of governance; financial and actuarial analysis; and regulatory framework and requirements [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements].

Guideline 12 requires that when assessing whether a person is 'proper', the undertaking includes an assessment of that person's honesty and financial soundness based on relevant evidence regarding their character, personal behaviour and business conduct, including any criminal, financial, supervisory aspects regardless of jurisdiction [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-12-proper-requirements].

EIOPA requires that the undertaking applies fit and proper procedures to assess persons employed by the service provider to perform an outsourced key function [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0015__guideline-14-outsourcing-of-key-function]. The undertaking must also designate a person within the undertaking with overall responsibility for the outsourced key function who is fit and proper and possesses sufficient knowledge and experience to challenge the service provider's performance [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0015__guideline-14-outsourcing-of-key-function]. Additionally, the undertaking must cover its approach and processes for outsourcing in a written policy, including criteria for determining whether a function is critical, how a service provider is selected and assessed, details of the written agreement, and business contingency plans [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy]. If key functions are outsourced within the group, the responsible entity must document which functions relate to which legal entity and ensure performance is not impaired [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0046__intra-group-outsourcing].

EIOPA requires an undertaking to assess its ability to perform and manage the investment or activity, the risks specifically related to it and its impact on the undertaking's risk profile, the consistency of the investment with beneficiaries' and policyholders' interests and liability constraints, and the impact of the investment on the quality, security, liquidity, profitability, and availability of the whole portfolio [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0027__guideline-26-assessment-of-non-routine-i].

According to Guideline 18, the undertaking's risk management policy must cover at least the following with regard to underwriting and reserving risk [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving]:

*   The types and characteristics of the insurance business, such as the type of insurance risk the undertaking is willing to accept [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
*   How the adequacy of premium income to cover expected claims and expenses is to be ensured [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
*   The identification of the risks arising from the undertaking's insurance obligations, including embedded options and guaranteed surrender values in its products [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
*   How, in the process of designing a new insurance product and the premium calculation, the undertaking takes account of the constraints related to investments [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
*   How, in the process of designing a new insurance product and the premium calculation, the undertaking takes account of reinsurance or other risk mitigation techniques [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].

Munich Re aims to reduce financed GHG emissions from thermal coal investments by more than 35% by 2025 compared to the base year 2019 and plans to phase out thermal coal completely by 2040 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0075__decarbonisation-approach-to-investments]. The company has committed to completely phase out thermal coal insurance by 2040 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities].

Munich Re will not accept stand-alone covers for Arctic oil and gas drilling activities, including directly related infrastructure and installations, nor mixed covers and treaty reinsurance business where the exposure is above a defined single-digit percentage threshold [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas].

Munich Re stopped insuring single-risk, single-location projects for new oil and gas fields on 1 April 2023 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas]. A field qualifies as 'new' if no prior production had taken place as of 31 December 2022 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].

Munich Re has developed a five-step analysis based on the TCFD recommendations: 1) Scenario identification, 2) Risk heat maps, 3) Impact tree/risk cluster, 4) Deep-dive critical risk cluster, and 5) Metrics [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0149__identifying-assessing-and-responding-to].

Munich Re has committed to transitioning its underwriting portfolio to net-zero GHG emissions by 2050 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities]. The Climate Ambition 2025 is a strategy that sets specific milestone targets up until 2050, with 2025 marking a crucial first milestone, and creates a holistic framework encompassing emissions associated with assets, liabilities, and own operations [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0150__decarbonisation-targets-and-metrics].

Swiss Re's Climate Transition Plan (CTP) is a document required by the Swiss Ordinance on Climate Disclosures that outlines the company's transition approach and action plan for decarbonising its business while developing related opportunities [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. It describes Swiss Re's net-zero transition approach and corresponding action plan [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0207__climate-transition-plan]. The transition approach is centred on a decarbonisation strategy that includes three key levers: jointly transition by engaging with clients, investees and vendors; manage portfolios; and reduce operational emissions [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. The action plan builds on Swiss Re's existing climate targets and the mitigating actions that have already been implemented across underwriting, investments and operations [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan].

Swiss Re does not provide re/insurance to companies or projects with more than 30% exposure to thermal coal for single-risk business [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]. Additionally, the policy restricts thermal coal assets of companies planning to develop new thermal coal mining sites or power capacity of at least 100MW for single-risk business [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]. Exceptions apply for transactions located in low- and middle-income countries that derive more than 70% of electricity from coal, provided the plant was operational before 2018 and the insured is implementing an effective emission reduction strategy [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].

Swiss Re does not support single-risk re/insurance transactions for oil and gas companies that produce the world's 10% most carbon-intensive oil and gas, measured as CO2 lifecycle emissions per barrel, based on Rystad data [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting]. It also declines to support standalone upstream projects within new oil and gas fields (those receiving a final investment decision after 2022) and standalone upstream oil and gas projects located in the Arctic Monitoring and Assessment Programme (AMAP) region (excluding Norway) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting]. Additionally, the company does not support oil and gas companies with more than 10% of upstream production located in the AMAP region (excluding Norway) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].
Swiss Re's ESG Risk Framework comprises three umbrella guidelines and six sector-specific policies [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0124__umbrella-guidelines-and-sector-specific].

Swiss Re uses an ESG risk assessment tool to evaluate potential transactions, typically single-risk business, classifying them as low, medium, or high risk, or giving a direct recommendation to abstain [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment]. High-risk transactions require a mandatory ESG risk referral to internal sustainability risk managers for an in-depth analysis [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. These managers assess the transaction's alignment with the ESG Risk Framework using public sources and third-party research, applying a 5% materiality threshold [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. The outcome is a binding recommendation to proceed, proceed with conditions, or abstain [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. A recommendation to abstain or proceed with conditions can be appealed and escalated to the next management level, with the decision-making authority remaining with Risk Management and the Group Chief Risk Officer [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process].

An impact tolerance is defined as the maximum tolerable level of disruption to an important business service, measured by a length of time in addition to any other relevant metrics [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0004__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf00].

After Monday 31 March 2025, maintaining operational resilience will be a dynamic activity, and firms should have sound, effective, and comprehensive strategies, processes, and systems to address risks to their ability to remain within their impact tolerance for each important business service in the event of a severe but plausible disruption [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0006__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf02].

Munich Re excludes companies generating more than 30% of revenue from thermal coal, while companies with 15% to 30% are either excluded or subject to engagement [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0100__defined-exclusion-criteria]. Swiss Re excludes companies generating at least 5% of revenue from thermal coal mining or power generation, with this threshold applying to corporate bonds with maturities beyond 2030 [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0260__swiss-re-s-fossil-fuel-related-threshold]. Additionally, Swiss Re restricts investments in the 10% most carbon-intensive oil and gas companies [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks].

Munich Re's approach to decarbonization is not explicitly detailed in the provided sources. [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0117__3-3-climate-related-disclosure]

Swiss Re's approach is centred on a decarbonisation strategy that includes three key levers: jointly transition by engaging with clients, investees and vendors; manage portfolios; and reduce operational emissions. [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]

Munich Re monitors climate-related risks by differentiating between physical and transition risks, which are integrated into its risk management system [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0126__climate-change-risk-drivers]. It applies different time horizons for assessment, including short-term (up to 2 years), medium-term (3-10 years), and long-term (more than 10 years) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0125__climate-change-time-horizons].

Swiss Re monitors climate-related risks by identifying industries and companies most exposed to transition risks through the assessment of GHG emissions and managing these risks via portfolio reallocations [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks]. It also applies forward-looking indicators, such as temperature scores, to its corporate bond portfolio to assess how a company's emissions and net-zero transition plans may translate into global warming [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks].

Munich Re highlights insurance solutions for ESG risks, which create a potential positive impact for society and the environment, and supports the Paris climate targets by insuring new, climate-friendly technologies to make them more attractive and financially viable for investors [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0062__aiming-for-positive-impact-through-insur][munich_re_sustainability_2023__0048__3-sustainability-in-business].

Swiss Re highlights re/insurance solutions for renewable energy projects, battery energy storage systems, and direct air capture technology, as well as a first-of-its-kind insurance for carbon credit forwards [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition].
I cannot answer this from the provided sources.

Swiss Re's underwriting approach aligns with PRA expectations by considering the impact of climate change on material underwriting portfolios and strategy, which is a key component of effective risk identification and assessment [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0202__underwriting][pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. The company regularly adjusts its proprietary probabilistic natural catastrophe models to reflect changing physical risks, a practice consistent with the PRA's expectation that insurers consider the latest climate science and advances in climate scenario modelling [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0202__underwriting][pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. Additionally, Swiss Re's climate scenario analyses focus on key exposures, such as tropical cyclones in the US and floods in the UK, which supports the PRA's expectation that insurers build on scenarios selected for stress and scenario testing to explore climate-related risks to their business model [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0202__underwriting][pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].

Munich Re invests in renewable energy, certified real estate, forests with certified management, and green bonds, with a significant allocation to renewable energy (€3.1bn) and forestry (€1.9bn) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0135__esg-focused-investments][munich_re_sustainability_2023__0135__esg-focused-investments]. Swiss Re offers re/insurance solutions for renewable energy projects, including wind, solar, hydro, geothermal, marine/tidal, and biomass, and wrote single-risk re/insurance for 21,573 wind and solar power plants in 2024, a 45% increase from the previous year [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0220__opportunities]. Additionally, Swiss Re invests in renewable energy debt, with 55% in solar and 45% in wind farms, and has a target to have a green, social, and sustainability bond portfolio of at least USD 4 billion [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0291__climate-solution-investments-green-bonds].
I cannot answer this from the provided sources.

Firms should have robust frameworks for risk management, including for financial and operational risks, to effectively identify, measure, monitor, and report risks [pra_ss5-25_climate__0020__business-strategy]. They must regularly carry out risk assessments to identify material climate-related risks, understand how these risks affect business model resilience over relevant time horizons, and consider transmission channels through which physical and transition risks impact risk types [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment]. Firms should also consider appropriate time horizons for impact analysis, including market expectations, maturities, business model adaptation timescales, and the expected run-off of insurance liabilities [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment]. The board should understand the capabilities and limitations of models and toolkits, conduct sensitivity analysis where appropriate, and consider model and input data uncertainty when interpreting results [pra_ss5-25_climate__0031__scenario-governance-controls-and-review]. Firms should ensure the board has an adequate understanding of scenario analysis exercises, including inputs, assumptions, design, outputs, and sources of uncertainty, to interpret results with appropriate context [pra_ss5-25_climate__0031__scenario-governance-controls-and-review]. Finally, firms should ensure that all material climate-related risks are included in the firm risk register and reported to the board, and that they are appropriately captured in risk appetite statements [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].

The PRA expects firms to identify, quantify, and evaluate climate-related risks that may materialise within their capital planning horizons and incorporate these into their Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) and stress testing programmes [pra_ss5-25_climate__0040__internal-capital-adequacy-assessment-pro]. For liquidity, firms must develop processes to identify, quantify, and evaluate risks that may impair their liquidity and funding positions, including the Internal Liquidity Adequacy Assessment Process (ILAAP) [pra_ss5-25_climate__0041__internal-liquidity-adequacy-assessment-p].

Specifically, firms should include an assessment of how they have determined material exposures to climate-related risks, providing sufficient detail of methodologies, scenarios, assumptions, judgements, and proxies [pra_ss5-25_climate__0040__internal-capital-adequacy-assessment-pro][pra_ss5-25_climate__0041__internal-liquidity-adequacy-assessment-p]. They must also assess the impact of these risks on net cash outflows and the value of assets comprising liquidity buffers to inform the level of liquidity they should hold [pra_ss5-25_climate__0041__internal-liquidity-adequacy-assessment-p]. Firms should use climate scenario analysis (CSA) as a key tool for these assessments [pra_ss5-25_climate__0040__internal-capital-adequacy-assessment-pro] and ensure their assessments consider appropriate time horizons [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].

Munich Re's decarbonization approach focuses on the further decarbonization of its asset management, (re)insurance business, and own business operations [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0015__ambition-2025]. The company's overall sustainability ambition is defined by its Group Ambition 2025 programme, which is built around three guiding principles: Scale, Shape, and Succeed [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0015__ambition-2025].

Munich Re has set long-term and mid-term targets to reduce the GHG emissions of insured activities related to thermal coal extraction and production, and coal-fired power plants by 35% by 2025 compared to the 2019 base year [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities]. The company has also committed to completely phasing out thermal coal insurance by 2040 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities].

Regarding oil and gas, Munich Re has committed to reducing its self-calculated GHG emissions associated with its business by 5% by 2025 relative to the 2019 base year [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities]. The company applies underwriting restrictions and exclusions for fossil fuels, including restrictions on new thermal coal mines, power plants, and infrastructure, as well as new oil and gas fields, new midstream oil infrastructure, oil-fired power plants, oil sands, and Arctic oil and gas activities [munich_re_sustainability_2024__0263__sustainability-report-2024]. Since 1 April 2023, Munich Re has ceased to insure contracts or projects on a single-risk basis covering the planning, financing, construction, or operation of new oil and gas fields, new midstream infrastructure, or new oil-fired power plants [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].

Swiss Re combines its underwriting approach with broader climate risk monitoring by actively monitoring and managing re/insurance portfolios exposed to physical and transition risks [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0227__underwriting]. It determines the relevance of these risks using quantitative thresholds (5% of total premium, reserves, or expected profits) and qualitative factors, including weather-related perils and annual expected losses for natural catastrophes [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0227__underwriting]. The company evaluates projected trends for these risks on both qualitative and quantitative bases [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0227__underwriting]. Additionally, it leverages research and publications to inform client conversations and external dialogues on adaptation and resilience [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0167__publications].

The PRA expects firms to identify material climate-related risks, including credit risks, through a two-step assessment process. This involves assessing transmission channels to understand how physical and transition risks impact risk types such as credit, and determining which combinations materially impact the firm's business model [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment]. Firms should also consider the appropriate time horizons for impact analysis, including the maturities of financial instruments and counterparty refinancing risks [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment]. Additionally, the PRA expects banks to support the timely recognition of climate-related risks in their financial statements, including credit risks, by giving due consideration to the application of materiality in applicable accounting standards [pra_ss5-25_climate__0035__financial-reporting].

Munich Re has issued three green bonds: a US$ 1.25bn bond in 2022, and two euro-denominated bonds with volumes of €1bn (2021) and €1.25bn (2020) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0109__munich-re-s-issued-green-bonds]. The total volume of green bonds issued was €2.25bn and US$1.25bn as of 31.12.2023 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0110__green-bonds].

The net proceeds are fully allocated to eligible projects in accordance with the Green Bond Framework [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0109__munich-re-s-issued-green-bonds]. The Green Bond Committee governs the selection and monitoring of these projects, reviewing the list annually to ensure compliance with eligibility criteria and exclusions [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0109__munich-re-s-issued-green-bonds][munich_re_sustainability_2023__0110__green-bonds].

Munich Re commits to publishing an allocation and impact report one year after issuance and annually thereafter until full allocation [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0110__green-bonds]. The company also invests in green bonds linked to renewable energies, sustainable waste management, and other sustainable sectors [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0106__renewable-energy-and-green-bonds].

Swiss Re's single-risk reinsurance is typically used for larger commercial assets that extend beyond what is covered by a treaty reinsurance contract [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0218__single-risk-re-insurance]. The company offers re/insurance solutions for renewable energy projects, battery energy storage systems, and direct air capture technology [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition]. In 2024, Swiss Re wrote single-risk re/insurance for 21,573 wind and solar power plants and 329 other renewable power plants, including hydropower, geothermal, marine/tidal, and biomass [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition]. The company also assesses clients' transition status to prioritize engagement on net-zero alignment [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0218__single-risk-re-insurance].
